322a59b7-e7b2-8135-914a-d27356d202ab.title: SouthLight Services
322a59b7-e7b2-8135-914a-d27356d202ab.summary: SouthLight is experiencing fundamental revenue recognition issues caused by invoice creation date vs. billing cycle date misalignment between Rev.io and QuickBooks, resulting in charges posting to incorrect accounting periods. The team identified this during first-month reconciliation and is pursuing a sandbox test and QB expert consultation, but no definitive fix exists yet. Separately, payment syncing is creating duplicates in QB, so the team disabled it temporarily. Multiple manual adjustments and ongoing reconciliation work is required, indicating the client is not yet operationally self-sufficient in the billing workflow.

322a59b7-e7b2-8135-914a-d27356d202ab.blockers: 1) QuickBooks integration does not allow passing charge dates through to GL entries—only invoice created date is used, which contradicts Rev.io's ability to assign charges to prior periods; requires QB expert consultation and possible system-level workaround. 2) Payment sync creating duplicate records in QuickBooks; temporary disable is band-aid. 3) Multiple invoice reversals and reissues due to tax corrections have created period misstatements that are difficult to unwind retroactively.
322a59b7-e7b2-8135-914a-d27356d202ab.keyFinding: Revenue recognition is fundamentally broken due to invoice creation date vs. billing cycle date mismatch between Rev.io and QuickBooks, causing period misstatements that cannot be easily corrected and will require ongoing manual intervention.

322a59b7-e7b2-81ed-94b2-d9e2b7f773b8.title: Cloudli
322a59b7-e7b2-81ed-94b2-d9e2b7f773b8.summary: Cloudli identified a critical revenue recognition bug: Rev.io is incorrectly creating new charges when payment refunds or chargebacks are processed, causing duplicate invoices to sync to NetSuite and inflating revenue. The team confirmed this is a Rev.io platform/configuration issue (not an integration mapping problem), and Cloudli has been manually deleting invoices and avoiding chargeback bookings entirely. Brendan Mahan and the Rev.io team acknowledged the problem requires investigation by Connor; meanwhile, Cloudli is stuck manually correcting 23+ transactions at month-end and unable to safely process their 4-5 monthly chargebacks. Next steps include Connor's direct engagement, a follow-up call, and investigation of secondary issues (missing tax data on credit memos, product item linking failures).

• Brendan Mahan: Investigate why Rev.io creates charges on payment refunds instead of reversing them; Investigate integration bug causing missing tax data on credit memos when default product type is used; Disable success notification emails and sandbox environment notifications to reduce alert noise
• Marshall Harris: Forward meeting recording and detailed refund/chargeback/commission issues to Connor for urgent review; Coordinate scheduling follow-up meeting with Connor for continued troubleshooting
• Jason Bunch: Provide clear examples of refund transactions and error emails to support troubleshooting; Raise commission upload inquiry with Connor
322a59b7-e7b2-81ed-94b2-d9e2b7f773b8.blockers: Critical: Rev.io platform behavior of creating charges on refunds is a design/configuration issue that requires Connor (Rev.io) to investigate and resolve; Cloudli unable to process chargebacks safely until resolved; Product item linking issues in credit memos preventing NetSuite sync; Missing tax data on credit memos blocks accurate revenue recognition
322a59b7-e7b2-81ed-94b2-d9e2b7f773b8.keyFinding: Rev.io is creating duplicate charges during refund/chargeback transactions, causing revenue to be double-booked in NetSuite, forcing Cloudli to manually delete 23+ invoices and halt chargeback processing entirely due to fear of compounding the error.

35da59b7-e7b2-8079-b354-f96afb2ce93d.summary: Nicole Hills walked Underdog Data through the data import methodology, offering API or Excel/CSV options. The team identified a critical data structure challenge: their system exports linked data (customers, locations, contacts, jobs) with multiple values collapsed into single cells, complicating import. They agreed on a layered, iterative approach—exporting data in separate batches, testing small imports first, and potentially using Claude AI to separate merged contact entries. Paul and Travis will export their 779-customer database with parent-child relationships preserved using existing account IDs. Nicole will provide the import template, attempt ingestion, and follow up on ADI integration pricing for automated product catalog and PO updates.

345a59b7-e7b2-8124-a213-c551e65b4ad9.title: VIA Technology
345a59b7-e7b2-8124-a213-c551e65b4ad9.summary: Nicole Hills led a comprehensive 108-minute PSA configuration discovery call with VIA Technology, covering product catalog setup, billing workflows, quote templates, ticketing, labor tracking, purchase orders, calendar/task management, and mobile geofencing. The team streamlined VIA's QuickBooks product list into generic billing categories (labor, materials, freight) and clarified that detailed project billing occurs in Textura with summary revenue rolled into PSA and QB monthly. A key tension emerged: VIA wants to shift from percentage-complete labor billing to hourly time tracking, but this conflicts with their Textura-driven workflow. Multiple configuration workstreams were assigned to Nicole (templates, billing rules, workflows, geofence, training), and vendor/provider data is pending from Patty. Client is engaged and prepared, but lacks hands-on PSA experience and clear resolution on the billing model transition.

• Mike: Review hybrid time tracking strategy (ticket-level logs + clock in/out) with Nicole (01:05:40)

345a59b7-e7b2-8124-a213-c551e65b4ad9.keyFinding: VIA Technology's billing workflow is tightly coupled to Textura (external AIA/pay application system) with no current integration, requiring manual monthly reconciliation of labor and materials into PSA—this creates a potential bottleneck for implementing hourly labor tracking as desired.

The team also discussed PO management (partial receipts, auto-updates), asset tracking, and transition to a centralized ticketing system with planned Alarm.com/UCC integrations; feasibility of these integrations is pending API sandbox assessment. Client engagement is strong but several action items require follow-up (Sedona IP, analytics reporting, integration testing, and CSR workflow alignment) before graduation readiness.

A critical risk was flagged: Tiger Paw access was mistakenly removed from users mid-week, and the team must prevent this from happening again until the official hard cutover date.

325a59b7-e7b2-816a-833b-fd37759281b6.summary: Newport Network Solutions held a working session focused on time tracking and payroll integration, but identified critical product gaps that led to a decision to pause implementation. The team discovered that the Rev.io system misclassifies admin time and PTO in utilization reports, lacks proper labor code and billable rate configuration, has an unreliable mobile app, and is missing core functionality needed for accurate billing and payroll. 337a59b7-e7b2-800f-961e-dee18b068878.title: Quick Start with Mule Security on 2026-05-06
Quick Start with Mule Security on 2026-04-14
337a59b7-e7b2-800f-961e-dee18b068878.notes:
Notes
System Configuration and User Management
The meeting laid a strong foundation for configuring system settings and user management to align with Mule Security’s operational needs.
• System Settings Setup and Defaults Configured for Organization (00:05)
    ◦ Mark detailed configuring system-wide settings including company info, business hours, time zones, and SMS notification options.
    ◦ SMS notifications for internal users were demoed but opted out for now by Robert; push notifications via desktop app were confirmed available and appreciated.
    ◦ Default ticket behaviors and settings were reviewed; Robert chose to keep “service call” as default ticket type and enable domain-based ticket assignment to capture emails without exact contact matches.
    ◦ Ticket activity notes explained as key communication logs techs enter, potentially visible to customers based on settings.
• User Management and Roles Established with Admin Assignments (00:45)
    ◦ Robert requested adding Kyle as a full admin user with all permissions, which Mark configured immediately.
    ◦ Role and permission structures were explained, including admin, standard user, and field tech roles.
    ◦ User groups were discussed for organizing tech roles, with initial creation attempted but encountered a system error; Mark recommended submitting a support ticket for resolution.
    ◦ Reporting access pending database setup for analytics integration scheduled closer to launch.
• Email System Integration and Validation Process Outlined (00:19)
    ◦ Setup of support inbox, outgoing email, and workflow email addresses was covered; Robert chose to use office@mulesecurity.com for support and outgoing mail.
    ◦ Mark explained DKIM/DMARC email validation via DNS record updates in GoDaddy to ensure proper email sending, with detailed instructions and SendGrid validation email forwarded to Robert’s team.
    ◦ Email reply reopening of closed tickets and spam quarantine rules overviewed but deferred for later configuration.
Customer and Contact Management
Customer data structure and contact management features were explored to support billing, communication, and operational workflows.
• Customer Records and Parent-Child Account Hierarchy Explained (00:50)
    ◦ Robert’s existing parent-child account model from Alarm Biller was mapped to PSA’s parent and child accounts using matching account IDs.
    ◦ Mark stressed careful assignment of parent accounts as it cannot be undone except by deleting and recreating customers.
    ◦ Customers can have multiple addresses with address types, allowing separation of billing and service locations.
    ◦ Multiple billing contacts can be added to receive invoices; Mark committed to confirm if multiple billing contacts trigger multiple invoice emails automatically.
• Portal User Access and Customer Interaction Via Portal (00:54)
    ◦ Portal user creation was demonstrated for Robert, enabling customers to view invoices, make payments, open tickets, and manage contacts.
    ◦ Password complexity requirements for portal access were noted as strict but manageable.
    ◦ Multiple portal users can be linked per customer to allow broad access.
• Customer Notes, Tags, and Document Attachments Supported (00:35)
    ◦ Notes can be flagged as pop-ups to alert users immediately upon accessing a customer record.
    ◦ Tags were confirmed useful for categorizing customers by service types like burglar alarm, fire alarm, etc.
    ◦ Documents can be uploaded to customer profiles to store quotes, installation photos, and other relevant files.
Product Catalog and Inventory Management
The product catalog and inventory system were reviewed with practical demonstrations for product entry, inventory tracking, and location management.
• Product Catalog Structured Around Four Key Types (01:26)
    ◦ Products categorized as labor, non-serialized, recurring service, or serialized for tax and tracking purposes.
    ◦ Attributes such as price, cost, manufacturer, UPC, and tax code overrides explained.
    ◦ Price adjustment enabled to allow techs to apply discounts on the fly.
    ◦ Option to convert sold products into customer assets for lifecycle tracking.
• Inventory Management Disabled Initially with Future Enablement Planned (01:17)
    ◦ Inventory tracking currently off per Robert’s priority; Mark demonstrated enabling inventory with valuation methods: standard, average, and specific.
    ◦ Serial number tracking explained as beneficial for precise asset valuation and transfer tracking.
    ◦ Locations set up as warehouse and trucks, with inventory transfer functionality demonstrated to balance stock across locations.
    ◦ Barcode scanner compatibility highlighted for efficient serial number entry.
• Purchase Orders and Providers Integration Reviewed (01:40)
    ◦ Providers added (e.g., ADI) but product-provider linking showed a bug preventing product selection for purchase orders.
    ◦ Purchase order process demoed including product addition, approval, emailing PO to vendors, receiving inventory, and cost adjustment.
    ◦ Mark described how purchase orders tie into inventory receiving and serial number management.
Calendar Synchronization and Task Scheduling
Calendar and task integration were introduced to streamline technician scheduling and operational visibility.
• Outlook Calendar Integration Setup Attempted with Admin Access Needed (01:10)
    ◦ Mark guided Robert through PSA Outlook calendar integration setup requiring admin permission in Microsoft Entra.
    ◦ Robert struggled with admin authentication; Mark recommended Kyle (confirmed admin) perform initial consent.
    ◦ Integration expected to sync calendar events bidirectionally between Outlook and PSA, enhancing scheduling accuracy.
• Calendar and Dispatch Board Features Highlighted (01:10)
    ◦ PSA calendar shows user-specific tasks and events with ability to assign calendar items to techs.
    ◦ Dispatch board allows drag-and-drop assignment of tickets to tech calendars, supporting workload balancing.
    ◦ Task list view enables easy management of assigned work.
Quoting, Opportunities, and Sales Pipeline
Quoting and opportunity management capabilities were reviewed to support sales workflows and customer proposal tracking.
• Quote Creation and Sending Demonstrated with Template Use (01:50)
    ◦ Mark created a one-off quote using existing templates, adding test products and assigning signature responsibilities.
    ◦ Sending quote triggers customer email with link to view and sign digitally; system tracks quote views and completions.
    ◦ Tax code setup pending to fully calculate quote totals.
• Opportunities Pipeline and Sales Stage Tracking Explained (01:54)
    ◦ Opportunities have six stages from qualification to closing, allowing sales progress tracking.
    ◦ Robert’s instance on older version; Mark offered to enable newer version with enhanced quote attachment functionality.
    ◦ Users can add notes, change stages, and track expected amounts and close dates within opportunities.
• Strategic Sales Process Adoption Encouraged (01:56)
    ◦ Mark encouraged starting with streamlined quote processes before moving to advanced pipeline features.
    ◦ Sales team can efficiently manage proposals and customer communications within PSA to improve closure rates.
Next Steps and Data Import Strategy
Clear next steps were established to move from setup to operational readiness through data imports and continuous learning.
• Data Preparation and Import Focus on Products and Customers (01:57)
    ◦ Robert to fill customer and product lists using Alarm Biller exports, ensuring product types align with PSA categories.
    ◦ Parent-child account ID mapping clarified to maintain hierarchical customer structure during import.
    ◦ Contacts to be linked via matching account IDs to customers.
• Follow-up Training Scheduled and Resources Provided (02:01)
    ◦ Next call scheduled for one week later, same time, to continue setup and address questions.
    ◦ Mark to send recording of this session and additional documentation including email setup instructions.
    ◦ Robert encouraged to explore system tables and configurations independently to build familiarity.
• Operational Adjustments Discussed for System Differences (02:16)
    ◦ Robert noted differences in system design compared to Alarm Biller, especially around customer sites and system distinctions.
    ◦ Emphasis on adapting workflows to PSA’s parent-child account model and address management.
    ◦ Ongoing support planned to help Mule Security transition smoothly from legacy systems to PSA platform.

32da59b7-e7b2-81db-af3f-d56571a8462f.title: Quick Start with QuickCopper on 2026-04-16
32da59b7-e7b2-81db-af3f-d56571a8462f.notes:
Notes
Ticketing System Setup and Usage
The team focused on learning and optimizing the use of the Rev IO PSA ticketing system, with emphasis on ticket creation, filtering, and macros to improve efficiency.
• Multiple Ticket Creation Methods Clarified (01:32)
    ◦ Tickets can be created via the ticket list, customer servicing tab, AI tool Revi, customer portal, or email support inbox.
    ◦ The main workflow for the team is creating tickets from the ticket list by clicking "Add Ticket."
    ◦ Mark Kephart emphasized logging in and following along to learn the system hands-on.
• Role and Permission Adjustments Made (04:50)
    ◦ Admin and global admin roles were assigned to Sean, Kelly, and Sal to resolve 403 forbidden access errors.
    ◦ Standard user roles were assigned to the rest, with flexibility to adjust permissions as needed.
    ◦ Global admin status resolved prior access issues quickly, enabling full system navigation.
• Ticket View Customization Encouraged (06:18)
    ◦ Users were advised to create personal filters for tickets by setting the primary technician to themselves and saving views for easier management.
    ◦ Views can be private or shared with specific groups to suit team collaboration preferences.
    ◦ This practice helps users track their active tickets efficiently.
• Macros Introduced to Automate Ticket Data Entry (08:53)
    ◦ Macros can pre-fill ticket fields like status, priority, contact, and work requested to save time.
    ◦ Mark demonstrated creating a test macro that assigned Daniel as primary tech with preset details.
    ◦ This approach reduces repetitive data entry and standardizes ticket creation.
• Activity Notes and Email Communication Explained (13:30)
    ◦ Tickets support public and internal activity notes; public notes can be emailed to customers or viewed in the customer portal.
    ◦ Attachments such as PDFs or images can be added to notes but video formats are unsupported.
    ◦ Proper email configuration is needed for notes to send automatically; currently pending setup.
• Time Tracking Features Detailed (20:46)
    ◦ Technicians can start/stop timers on tickets to log work and travel time, supporting up to three concurrent timers.
    ◦ Time logs can be marked billable; a new status like "Review for Billing" is suggested to trigger alerts to billing staff.
    ◦ Time entries appear on timesheets with calculated utilization based on an eight-hour workday.
• Task and Calendar Integration Previewed (26:27)
    ◦ Tasks can be assigned to team members with priority and linked to calendar events for scheduling.
    ◦ The system will integrate with Outlook calendars, syncing tasks and events once configured.
    ◦ Users can view others' availability before assigning tasks, enabling better resource management.
• Purchase Order Process and Vendor Data Needs Identified (30:44)
    ◦ Purchase orders can be created from tickets with selected products, shipping details, and vendor info.
    ◦ Current vendor lists are incomplete; Mark will request vendor and product data migration from Tiger Paw to Rev IO.
    ◦ Non-stock items are not supported in purchase orders but can be added to proposals, which generate tickets after approval.
Customer Portal and Multi-Location Account Management
The group addressed customer portal functionalities and critical gaps in parent-child account ticket visibility.
• Customer Portal Capabilities Reviewed (46:30)
    ◦ Portal users can view tickets, submit new ones, see activity notes with attachments, and make payments.
    ◦ Payments can be set up with bank info and auto-pay; payment history is accessible.
    ◦ Contact management is view-only on the app but editable in the portal.
• Parent-Child Account Integration Issue Highlighted (48:01)
    ◦ Current portal setup does not allow parent accounts to view tickets from child accounts, creating multiple logins for large clients with many locations.
    ◦ Mark verified that parents see only their own tickets, not child accounts' tickets.
    ◦ The team requested enabling parent accounts to see all child account tickets, with preference that children do not see other accounts.
• Invoices and Payments Align with Parent Accounts (53:16)
    ◦ Unlike tickets, invoices and payments from child accounts are consolidated and visible under parent accounts.
    ◦ This contrast underscores the importance of fixing ticket visibility for parent accounts.
• Mark Committed to Investigate and Request Feature Enhancement (49:58)
    ◦ Mark will pursue enabling parent account ticket visibility with the product team, aiming to meet the client’s operational needs.
Mobile Application for Field Technicians
The team explored the Rev IO mobile app to improve field ticket management and technician responsiveness.
• Mobile App Features and Login Process Explained (59:24)
    ◦ The app supports calendar view, ticket lists filtered by assigned technician, and customer info browsing.
    ◦ Users log in using the same credentials as the web platform without needing the full URL prefix.
    ◦ Face ID and biometric login are supported, confirmed by Daniel’s test.
• Ticket Handling and Location-Based Timer Automation (01:05:46)
    ◦ The app uses GPS geofencing to automatically start timers when technicians arrive within 200 feet of a job site.
    ◦ Users can email contacts directly from the app and view ticket details, including activity feeds and attachments.
    ◦ Customer signature capture is enabled on mobile for job completion verification.
• On-Call and After-Hours Notifications Discussed (01:08:49)
    ◦ The app sends push notifications for assigned tickets, confirmed by Sean receiving an alert on his phone.
    ◦ Unassigned tickets do not trigger notifications by default; workflows can be configured to notify a catch-all contact.
    ◦ The team emphasized the need for scheduled notifications after hours and weekends to support on-call technicians.
• Ticket Creation via Mobile App and AI Tool Revi (01:19:42)
    ◦ Technicians can create tickets on the spot from the app to ensure work is tracked promptly.
    ◦ Revi AI is designed to create tickets and revise message text, but current bugs prevent full functionality; a fix is expected soon.
    ◦ Mark will coordinate with the AI team to restore voice recognition ticket creation.
Workflow Automation and Notifications
Discussion focused on setting up workflows to automate ticket notifications and streamline triage.
• Workflow Creation for Ticket Alerts in Progress (01:22:50)
    ◦ Mark demonstrated building a workflow to notify technicians via text when tickets are created without assigned customers.
    ◦ Text and email are the primary notification channels supported; Microsoft Teams notifications are also possible.
    ◦ The group acknowledged Kelly manages text workflows, and Mark will collaborate to finalize effective alerts for techs.
• Challenges with Contact Information for Notifications Noted (01:25:57)
    ◦ Notification workflows require up-to-date tech contact info; the team recognized the need to maintain accurate phone numbers for texts.
    ◦ Mark plans further review and testing of workflows to ensure proper delivery of alerts.
Integration and Data Migration from Tiger Paw
The team identified key integration needs to synchronize products, vendors, and proposal items from Tiger Paw to Rev IO.
• Vendor and Product Data Migration Required (31:35)
    ◦ Tiger Paw contains the comprehensive vendor list and product catalog not fully present in Rev IO.
    ◦ Mark committed to requesting a one-time import of vendors and products to complete the Rev IO database before go-live.
    ◦ The migration is not designed as a continuous sync; updates added before go-live need manual entry or reimport.
• Non-Stock Items Handling in Proposals and POs Under Review (36:52)
    ◦ Tiger Paw supports non-stock items in proposals and purchase orders; Rev IO currently limits non-stock to proposal use only.
    ◦ Mark will test how non-stock items flow from proposals to tickets and whether they can be incorporated into purchase orders.
    ◦ This is important for handling customized packages and one-off items during sales and fulfillment.
Email Configuration and Technical Blockers
Resolving email setup issues is critical to enabling ticket communications and full system functionality.
• Email Integration Issues Persist with Support Inbox (07:57)
    ◦ Test emails to the support inbox fail validation and do not route into tickets as expected.
    ◦ Sean and Daniel have configured IMAP and DNS settings, but errors continue, blocking inbound email processing.
• Troubleshooting Steps Being Taken with Expert Support (01:35:32)
    ◦ Daniel detailed running PowerShell commands to connect Exchange Online and authenticate with global admin credentials.
    ◦ Despite correct setup steps, sign-in logs show no activity, indicating the mailbox is not accessed by Rev IO.
    ◦ The team plans to delete and recreate app registrations and secrets to resolve authentication issues.
• Next Steps for Email Resolution Planned (01:44:59)
    ◦ Mark will coordinate with Darren (email expert) to diagnose and fix the problem.
    ◦ Until email works, full end-to-end testing cannot proceed.
    ◦ A follow-up meeting is scheduled to finalize email fixes and prepare for go-live.
Go-Live Planning and Training
The team aligned on timelines and next steps for moving into production.
• Target Go-Live Date Set for Wednesday, March 22nd (01:28:20)
    ◦ The team agreed to start using the system live on that Wednesday after email functionality is confirmed.
    ◦ A support meeting is planned for Thursday from 10:00 to 11:00 to answer questions and troubleshoot issues during initial use.
• Encouragement to Test and Provide Feedback (01:31:33)
    ◦ Users were urged to explore the system, note likes, dislikes, and missing features.
    ◦ Mark and Sean will address issues in the follow-up call and adjust workflows or permissions as needed.
    ◦ Early hands-on testing is critical to smooth adoption and problem resolution prior to full deployment.

32da59b7-e7b2-81db-af3f-d56571a8462f.title: Quick Start with QuickCopper on 2026-04-13
32da59b7-e7b2-81db-af3f-d56571a8462f.notes:
Notes
User Management and Access Setup
The team focused on resolving login and user management issues to ensure smooth access to the new PSA platform for Quick Copper Technologies.
• Instance Access and Login Issues Identified and Addressed (00:12)
    ◦ Sean Brown reported inability to access the updated instance named Quick Hopper tech.
    ◦ Mark Kephart explained complexities in instance renaming and provided an existing instance named Quick Copper tied to billing credentials.
    ◦ Despite attempts, Sean experienced persistent redirects to the billing platform, prompting Mark to escalate the issue to the instance creation team.
    ◦ A plan was made to spin up a new instance with the correct naming and linkage to billing to avoid confusion and login problems.
• User Creation and Role Management Clarified (09:08)
    ◦ Users with billing logins automatically appear in PSA; no need to create new users manually.
    ◦ Roles and permissions can be adjusted post-login, with typical roles being admin, center user, and technician.
    ◦ Mark recommended making Sean, Kelly, and Sal global admins for comprehensive system access.
    ◦ User groups can be assigned to tickets to share visibility without assigning responsibility to all group members.
• Single Sign-On and Shared Database Confirmed (19:58)
    ◦ Tickets created in PSA are visible in billing due to shared database architecture.
    ◦ Customer portal logins differ from billing logins; Bill Center remains the preferred portal for customers.
    ◦ No need to create additional portal users for existing billing customers.
• Multi-Factor Authentication and Security Practices (02:35)
    ◦ Login process includes multi-factor authentication, enhancing security for user access.
Platform Configuration and System Settings
The session covered essential platform configuration details to align PSA with Quick Copper’s operational needs, highlighting some technical challenges with the current instance.
• Company Profile Setup and Bug Identification (29:20)
    ◦ Attempted input of company address, phone, and website revealed saving errors linked to an outdated instance version.
    ◦ Mark suspects the instance has not been updated due to inactivity; plans to wipe and reset the instance to a clean state were discussed.
    ◦ Importance of accurate company info stressed for calendar scheduling and SMS notification features.
• Ticket Defaults and Workflow Settings Established (36:45)
    ◦ Default ticket status set to “new open pending closed” with standard ticket types limited to four.
    ◦ Default priority and user group assignments can be configured to streamline ticket creation.
    ◦ Activity notes can be set to default to “email customer” to ensure communication is automatic and consistent.
• Time Entry and Labor Tracking Tailored (50:19)
    ◦ Labor billing is not applicable; labor is included in monthly recurring charges.
    ◦ The team decided to track technician time for internal reporting without billing impact.
    ◦ A "$0 admin labor tracking" product was created to enable time tracking with no billing consequences.
    ◦ Time entries will default to non-billable to avoid accidental invoicing.
• Email Integration and Workflow Triggers Planned (42:16)
    ◦ Email inboxes for ticket creation will be configured via Microsoft Entra credentials.
    ◦ Outbound emails for tickets and workflow notifications will use support@quickcopper.net.
    ◦ Spam filtering and quarantine features will help manage inbound ticket emails.
    ◦ Sean committed to setting up a test email account for initial validation before live deployment.
Ticketing and Service Workflow
The team reviewed ticket management functionality with a focus on ease of use and operational alignment for Quick Copper’s service delivery.
• Ticket Creation and Macro Usage Explained (01:00:50)
    ◦ Macros allow technicians to quickly fill in common ticket fields, reducing manual entry.
    ◦ Example macros include service calls and password resets to speed up ticket processing.
    ◦ Tickets can be filtered and viewed by status, technician, or other criteria, enhancing workload management.
• Ticket Detail Handling and Communication (01:17:28)
    ◦ Public notes are emailed to customers automatically; internal notes remain confidential.
    ◦ Contacts linked to customers can be chosen for email communication on tickets.
    ◦ Attachments and screenshots can be added directly to tickets for better issue documentation.
    ◦ Signature capture is possible both in the web app and mobile app for completed service validation.
• Task Assignment and Calendar Integration (01:37:22)
    ◦ Tasks can be assigned to multiple users and appear as calendar blocks on both PSA and Outlook calendars.
    ◦ Tasks show status and link directly to related tickets or customers.
    ◦ The dispatch board allows managers to allocate tickets and view technician calendars for efficient scheduling.
• Purchase Orders Supported Independently or Ticket-Linked (01:40:20)
    ◦ Purchase orders (POs) can be created as standalone entries or linked directly to service tickets.
    ◦ Internal POs and vendor-based POs are both supported, with optional approval workflows.
    ◦ PO approval is primarily relevant for inventory receipt; Quick Copper currently does not track inventory actively.
Inventory and Product Management
Inventory management was discussed with caution given current operational practices, highlighting flexibility for future adoption.
• Inventory Management Disabled Initially (40:21)
    ◦ Quick Copper currently does not track inventory due to operational complexity and low adherence risks.
    ◦ Mark advised that turning inventory off after activation deletes all records permanently.
    ◦ The system supports three valuation methods: standard cost, average cost, and specific cost per serialized item.
• Product Catalog Usage Demonstrated (01:22:50)
    ◦ Inventory tracked products require reservation of serial numbers before billing.
    ◦ Parts and labor can be added to tickets for job costing and profit tracking even without active inventory management.
    ◦ Items added to tickets reflect cost and quantity for micro-level financial visibility.
Strategic Transition and Rollout Planning
The discussion confirmed a phased approach to adopting the PSA platform focused initially on ticketing functionality.
• Phased Rollout Focused on Ticketing (26:46)
    ◦ Quick Copper’s first phase is using PSA exclusively for ticketing and mobile app functionality.
    ◦ Sales quoting, inventory, and purchase orders will be addressed in later phases after initial adoption stabilizes.
    ◦ Sean emphasized minimal training on quoting now as sales users will remain on Tiger Paw until mid-year releases.
• User Training and Go-Live Timeline Set (01:49:51)
    ◦ Training for technicians planned for Thursday, contingent on successful instance setup and email integration.
    ◦ Sean requested a “go/no-go” decision by Wednesday 5 PM to confirm readiness for training and cutover.
    ◦ Mark offered to provide a half-hour setup assistance session on Wednesday if needed.
    ◦ A test email account will be used before switching to live mailboxes to prevent workflow disruption.
• Instance Reset and Naming Clarification (01:10:22)
    ◦ Mark is coordinating to have a new instance created with the name “Quick Copper Technologies” for PSA distinct from the billing instance named “Quick Copper.”
    ◦ This naming helps prevent user confusion and ensures clear separation between billing and PSA platforms.
    ◦ The new instance will be linked to the existing billing database for seamless data sharing.
Operational Insights and User Feedback
Sean shared practical insights and feedback on operational preferences and system usability.
• Labor Billing and Time Tracking Preferences (51:28)
    ◦ Labor is generally included in monthly contracts, with occasional one-time charges for projects like wiring runs.
    ◦ Time tracking is desired for internal accountability but not for billing purposes.
    ◦ Accurate, non-rounded time entries preferred over estimated or rounded values.
• Current System Limitations and User Frustrations (31:06)
    ◦ Mark and Sean noted issues with saving data and system stability in the current instance, reflecting older bugs.
    ◦ Sean stressed the importance of a stable, reliable platform without freezing or errors to avoid adoption hurdles.
    ◦ The team acknowledged the need for a clean, updated instance to ensure smooth operations.
• Competitive Positioning and Platform Evolution (01:47:48)
    ◦ Quick Copper is transitioning from Tiger Paw due to dissatisfaction with its ticketing and email integration.
    ◦ PSA’s web-based platform is seen as a strategic improvement but still evolving, especially in sales and quoting features.
    ◦ The gradual migration strategy allows for tuning and user adaptation while minimizing disruption.
• Special Customer Handling and Incident Response (01:35:39)
    ◦ Sean shared an example of handling FOIA/OPRA requests from public entities, emphasizing the importance of quick response to critical clients.
    ◦ This example highlights the need for efficient ticketing and communication workflows in the PSA platform.

32da59b7-e7b2-81db-af3f-d56571a8462f.title: QuickCopper Kickoff on 2026-04-06
32da59b7-e7b2-81db-af3f-d56571a8462f.notes:
Notes
Data Migration and Platform Integration
The team agreed on a full copy migration of all service tickets from Tiger Paw to the new web platform to avoid data loss and ensure continuity (02:35).
• The migration will include all service orders, even if some contain extraneous data, which can be cleaned up post-migration within the web system.
• Jeremy Adams emphasized staging and prepping the data before final push, allowing filtering and closing irrelevant tickets without affecting Tiger Paw data.
• The cutover will involve an initial full data capture followed by a delta update to sync changes before the hard switch.
Linking the PSA web instance directly to the existing billing instance was prioritized to avoid duplicate customer entries and streamline customer creation (05:39).
• Jeremy confirmed new customers created in billing will automatically appear in the PSA system, reducing manual data entry.
• Shared customer and contact data will sync between billing and the web platform, though ticketing remains isolated to the web version due to structural differences.
The team discussed the importance of parent-child account visibility for customers with multiple locations, aiming for a unified login experience by the June release (07:21).
• Jeremy will verify the current support for parent-child accounts in the customer portal and confirm what data customers can view across child accounts.
• This feature is new and not currently available but is considered valuable for customer usability and centralized ticket oversight.
Onboarding and Training Timeline
The goal is to complete onboarding and full PSA setup by end of April, with training sessions starting the week of April 13th (30:11).
• Mark Kephart proposed a two-hour quick start session on Monday, April 13th, from 3 PM to 5 PM, targeting system settings, user roles, and permissions for the core team.
• A follow-up training session for technicians is scheduled for Thursday, April 16th, 10 AM to 12 PM, covering ticketing workflows and mobile app usage.
• Additional short sessions will follow to cover integrations and project management, aiming to have the system fully operational and ready for launch by the second to last week of April.
The client emphasized minimal need for basic onboarding as they are experienced users and mainly want access and cutover confirmation (12:55).
• Focus will be on practical training for project management and ticketing workflows rather than introductory content.
• The mobile app will be highlighted in technician training, as it is critical for on-call and remote workers to manage tickets on the go.
Product Features and Customization Needs
The team highlighted project management as a key feature lacking in current workflows, causing tracking and follow-through issues on new projects (17:11).
• Mark committed to emphasizing project management training to integrate ticketing and project timelines effectively.
• The client noted past attempts with SharePoint and other tools failed mainly due to lack of consistent use and training, seeking a solution that enforces workflow adherence.
Email integration with support@quickcopper.net will use API permissions through Entre for inbound and outbound ticket handling, improving over the current POP3 system (19:21).
• The new system will include spam filtering and safeguards against email loops that previously caused ticket flooding when replying to external ticket systems.
• Improved domain matching will help auto-assign tickets to the correct customer accounts, reducing manual assignment but not eliminating it where multiple matches exist.
Asset tracking was discussed as a potential future feature, especially for MSP customers needing serial and model number tracking (23:52).
• Adoption depends on ease of use and consistent procedural enforcement by technicians for scanning and adding assets.
• Mark will readdress asset management options later in the onboarding process if the client shows interest.
Contract, Licensing, and Billing Details
The contract includes eight standard licenses at $100 per seat, three Tiger Paw user licenses at $100 per seat, and eleven dual users at $50 per Tiger Paw and $50 per standard seat, totaling $2,200 per month (10:47).
• Billing start date is set for April 17th, aligned with the previous contract’s billing cycle to avoid proration or double billing.
• Blaine Villafuerte clarified that Tiger Paw access for users will remain until official cutover, preventing service disruption due to premature deactivation.
The client confirmed no customers exist in Tiger Paw that are not also in billing, simplifying integration and avoiding duplicate customer records (05:42).
• This alignment supports the decision to link PSA directly with billing for real-time customer data synchronization.
Instance Setup and User Access
A new PSA instance will be created under the name Quick Copper Technologies, replacing the old sandbox instance which is no longer accessible or connected properly to billing (27:28).
• Jeremy and Mark agreed to request a fresh instance with the correct naming to avoid user confusion and ensure proper billing linkage.
• The client prefers the instance name to reflect their current legal and marketing identity for internal and potential portal use.
User account setup will precede training, with all users receiving login credentials and early access to explore the system before cutover (37:16).
• The client expects to begin testing and familiarization as soon as the new instance is live, ideally by the end of the day following this meeting.
• A test email account (tp_support@quickcopper.net) will be set up to allow ticketing system trials separate from the live support email, reducing risk during transition.
Communication and Support Processes
Mark highlighted the help and support button within PSA for logging bugs or feature requests, which routes directly to the Rev IO support team via Zendesk (25:40).
• Users can attach screenshots or videos to clarify issues, ensuring faster resolution and ongoing platform improvements.
• The new Community app was introduced as a forum for sharing ideas and feature requests with the PSA development team, fostering user engagement in product evolution.
The AI assistant Revi inside PSA can automate tasks such as ticket creation, transfers, and reporting, offering productivity gains once users become familiar with it (28:22).
• Mark encouraged the team to explore Revi’s capabilities during onboarding to leverage automation and reduce manual work.
The client expressed appreciation for the efforts to simplify cutover and onboarding, emphasizing ease and speed as priorities to minimize disruption (41:22).
• Mark reassured the team the onboarding will proceed quickly and smoothly, with full support throughout the process.

326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.title: Quick Start with CMP Consulting  on 2026-04-14
326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.notes:
Notes
PSA Integration and Product Mapping
The team identified key gaps in product and customer mapping within the PSA integration, highlighting manual workarounds and data inconsistencies as critical blockers.
• Manual product mapping challenge with 344 items is slowing progress due to missing or zero SKU values in imports, forcing Carlos to manually copy-paste mappings (02:34)
    ◦ Imported product data from QuickBooks lacks SKUs, preventing auto-mapping
    ◦ Carlos is disabling unneeded products to reduce clutter and improve mapping accuracy
    ◦ Mark suggests sending updated SKU data for better import handling
    ◦ The manual approach is inefficient and error-prone, risking billing accuracy
• Customer duplication and missing data issues impact sync reliability between QuickBooks and Rev IO, creating confusion and double entries (15:55)
    ◦ Duplicate companies like Advisio and O’Naughton Brothers appear in Rev IO but not in QuickBooks
    ◦ Missing required fields such as address or email cause some customers to fail import
    ◦ Andrew confirms name, email, address, billing type, status, and bill profile are minimum requirements for sync
    ◦ Carlos is manually merging or deactivating duplicates to clean up the database
• API documentation and support ticketing gaps cause delays and confusion in resolving integration issues (11:39)
    ◦ Carlos notes documentation lacks practical examples like screenshots for OAuth2 permissions
    ◦ Support tickets do not generate initial confirmation emails, only responses trigger notifications
    ◦ Mark agrees to escalate and follow up on ticket assignment and notification issues with support team
    ◦ Lack of clear feedback loops hinders timely problem resolution
• Complex multi-SKU Microsoft 365 billing variants are unsupported by current PAX8 sync, limiting automation for different payment terms (10:58)
    ◦ Carlos explains multiple billing options (monthly, annual, three-year) under same SKU complicate invoicing
    ◦ PAX8 integration only pulls product catalog, not license counts or variant pricing
    ◦ Mark and Andrew acknowledge current integration doesn’t handle SKU variants or pricing tiers automatically
    ◦ Carlos manually assigns products and invoice line items to accommodate billing nuances, but this is not sustainable
    ◦ Discussion highlights need for enhanced integration to automatically recognize SKU variants and billing terms
Billing, Invoice Templates, and Provider Management
The discussion revealed ongoing work to improve invoice templates, billing profiles, and provider assignment functionality to better match customer needs and streamline operations.
• Invoice template enhancements to include work performed and ticket activities are imminent, addressing a major user request (01:59)
    ◦ Mark plans to enable invoice template customization by end of week per COO Evan’s commitment
    ◦ This feature will improve billing transparency and customer understanding of charges
    ◦ Strong community demand pressures the team to prioritize this update
• Provider assignment for products is partially supported but lacks recurring service integration, limiting purchase order and billing flexibility (33:14)
    ◦ Providers can be assigned to non-serialized and serialized products with cost differentiation
    ◦ Recurring services currently lack purchasing tab for provider assignment, a gap Mark commits to escalating for enhancement
    ◦ Carlos highlights scenarios where same product is sourced from multiple providers, requiring clear provider tagging
    ◦ This feature is expected to arrive soon based on user demand and internal requests
• Tax code assignment to product categories is straightforward but requires setup, ensuring compliance with state-specific tax rules (56:00)
    ◦ Mark will send Carlos a spreadsheet to match tax codes to non-serialized, serialized, recurring services, and labor categories
    ◦ Florida-specific tax rules apply, such as labor being taxable if billed with taxable hardware
    ◦ Proper tax code assignment will automate tax calculation on invoices
    ◦ Carlos acknowledges current simplicity but will review after receiving the spreadsheet
• Billing profiles and recurring service billing cycles offer flexibility for proration and billing frequency (58:10)
    ◦ Profiles define invoice timing, proration for partial periods, and billing intervals
    ◦ Recurring services can override billing profile defaults for custom cycles (e.g., annual billing)
    ◦ Mark demonstrates proration handling and billing start date management
    ◦ Carlos confirms understanding and plans to leverage these features
PAX8 and Licensing Integration Challenges
The PAX8 integration is a critical bottleneck due to limited support for billing variants and license synchronization, directly impacting MSP billing automation.
• PAX8 integration currently imports product catalog but not license counts or variant pricing, limiting automation of subscription billing (13:58)
    ◦ Carlos explains license counts update recurring invoices with prorated charges automatically
    ◦ However, multiple SKU variants for payment terms are not distinguished in Rev IO, complicating billing accuracy
    ◦ Mark notes current system treats all variants as one product, preventing automatic billing adjustments for different terms
    ◦ Carlos demonstrates adding licenses and syncing prorated charges manually, highlighting the workflow gap
• Potential for enhanced PAX8 integration to support SKU variants and billing nuances is under evaluation but timeline is uncertain (23:31)
    ◦ Andrew commits to taking feedback back to integration team for feasibility analysis
    ◦ Mark plans to clip meeting recording to communicate requirements clearly to product team
    ◦ Carlos emphasizes PAX8 integration as a top priority to overcome current roadblocks
    ◦ No promised delivery date, but the issue is recognized as critical for MSPs
• Parent-child customer relationships and billing aggregation are supported but require clear setup (03:41)
    ◦ Carlos describes billing child accounts directly and having invoices roll up to parent automatically
    ◦ Mark confirms this is standard PSA behavior, but nuances exist depending on accounting needs
    ◦ This impacts how subscriptions and licenses are invoiced across related entities
Quoting, Contract Management, and Customer Portal
The team explored using quoting tools to streamline contract generation and customer interactions, highlighting gaps in customer portal visibility.
• Quoting tool can be adapted for contract generation and e-signatures, supporting flexible business use beyond sales cycles (46:44)
    ◦ Carlos wants to generate simple text-based contracts or notices without sales funnel complexity
    ◦ Mark shows how recurring services added to quotes can become contracts after signing
    ◦ E-signature capability in quoting can formalize agreements and facilitate contract management
    ◦ Carlos plans to copy existing contract text into quote templates for reuse
• Customer portal currently lacks direct contract visibility, limiting customer access to signed agreements (52:35)
    ◦ Mark demonstrates portal features: ticket creation, invoice viewing, payments, and contact management
    ◦ Contracts can be attached to tickets created upon quote signing to provide access workaround
    ◦ Portal enhancements to show contracts natively are planned but not yet available
    ◦ This limits self-service capabilities for customers regarding contract review
• Price update propagation to recurring services is unclear and lacks configurable controls (40:14)
    ◦ Mark tests changing product prices but recurring service prices do not update automatically
    ◦ Carlos suggests a setting on the contract to control price update behavior per customer
    ◦ Mark agrees to add this feature request to community for prioritization
NinjaOne Integration and Asset Management
Integration with NinjaOne RMM is recognized as incomplete, with key needs around asset syncing, ticket automation, and alert handling driving future development.
• NinjaOne assets are not currently visible or fully synced in PSA, limiting alert and ticket context (06:05)
    ◦ Carlos wants tickets to link directly to NinjaOne assets for quick issue identification
    ◦ Mark confirms assets must exist in PSA first to be linked successfully
    ◦ Current NinjaOne integration focuses on customers and contacts, not assets or devices
• Ticket automation based on NinjaOne alerts requires sophisticated logic, including self-healing and condition resets (08:47)
    ◦ Carlos describes scenarios where alerts trigger tickets only if unresolved after delay or repeated occurrence
    ◦ Self-closed alerts (e.g. rebooted server) should close linked tickets automatically
    ◦ This reduces ticket noise and focuses tech attention on persistent issues
    ◦ Mark acknowledges complexity and confirms feasibility with defined logic rules
• No desktop agent for direct ticket creation exists currently within PSA, unlike NinjaOne’s RMM agent ticket creation (13:03)
    ◦ Carlos wants a simple client-side app or webhook for end users to submit tickets easily
    ◦ Mark explains current reliance on Zendesk for ticketing and ongoing plans to replace with native PSA system
    ◦ Desktop or web portal ticket creation would improve user experience and reduce email dependency
Process Improvements and Support System Issues
The meeting surfaced several process gaps in support communications, update visibility, and system usability affecting operational efficiency.
• Zendesk ticketing system lacks initial submission confirmation, causing uncertainty for users submitting support requests (08:42)
    ◦ Carlos never receives initial ticket confirmation emails, only responses
    ◦ Mark confirms this is expected behavior but plans to raise issue for improvement
    ◦ Misrouted tickets assigned to onboarding instead of support team cause delays in handling
• Integration update wizard shows persistent update alerts even after completion, confusing users (45:15)
    ◦ Carlos and Mark discuss update workflow and potential UI bugs causing update reminder to remain visible
    ◦ Andrew clarifies update process steps and confirms that version 30 is current
    ◦ Visual glitches may cause users to think updates are incomplete
• Duplicate product and customer entries complicate management, with no easy tool to identify or remove duplicates (42:36)
    ◦ Carlos disables duplicate products by renaming and unmapping to avoid confusion
    ◦ Mark suggests adding a duplicate detection feature to streamline cleanup
    ◦ Manual cleanup is time-consuming and error-prone, impacting data quality
• Class tracking in QuickBooks integration causes friction but is not required, avoiding unnecessary complexity (34:48)
    ◦ Carlos enabled class tracking but prefers to keep it off due to lack of usage
    ◦ Andy confirms integration does not heavily rely on class tracking
    ◦ This reduces overhead and simplifies billing configuration for users
Meeting Logistics and Next Steps
The team agreed on next sessions and follow-up actions to advance setup and address roadblocks.
• Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday 1:00 to 3:00 PM, focusing on billing system walkthrough and tax setup (02:02:21)
    ◦ Mark will send tax code spreadsheet for product category assignment before next call
    ◦ Carlos to continue cleaning up mappings and prepare questions for billing review
• Mark will escalate PAX8 integration limitations and provider assignment gaps to product team, leveraging recorded session segments for clarity (01:23:49)
    ◦ Priority set on enhancing license variant support and provider management on recurring services
    ◦ No fixed timeline but recognition of business impact ensures attention
• Support ticketing workflow and notification improvements are flagged for investigation to enhance user experience (04:09)
    ◦ Mark to check ticket routing and confirmation email logic with support operations
    ◦ Carlos prefers automatic initial ticket confirmation for transparency
• Community channel remains an active feedback platform, with Carlos encouraged to keep posting ideas and issues for visibility (16:55)
    ◦ Andy appreciates direct customer feedback for product planning
    ◦ Mark supports using community input to prioritize feature development

326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.title: Quick Start with CMP Consulting  on 2026-04-14
326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.notes:
Notes
Integration Data Import and Synchronization
The meeting focused heavily on resolving integration issues between QuickBooks Online (QBO), Rev PSA, and PAX8 to enable accurate data synchronization and mapping.
• Customer and Product Data Import Challenges centered on incomplete and inaccurate mapping between QBO and Rev PSA, with missing customer data and swapped product fields causing sync failures (00:42)
    ◦ Carlos confirmed QBO’s shallow copy approach complicates customer list accuracy, emphasizing the need for a clean export from QBO or Synchro for import (01:33)
    ◦ Mark and Carlos identified that product descriptions and SKUs were swapped in the import template, which likely blocked product mapping; a fix to switch these fields was agreed (35:38)
    ◦ The integration currently does not automatically handle new customers well, requiring manual imports to seed data for sync to work efficiently (24:59)
    ◦ Andrew recommended importing a customer list, then removing existing links before rerunning sync to generate more automatic mappings (01:02:14)
• Invoice and Payment Data Integration was discussed with respect to importing unpaid invoices and applying payments across systems (07:43)
    ◦ Invoice data depends on customer data being in place first, as invoices must attach to customers for sync (28:59)
    ◦ Andrew confirmed that once customers are imported and linked, related invoices should sync with all details intact, including status and charges (01:31:37)
    ◦ Carlos raised concerns about handling manual payments like wire or ACH and how those reflect in payment methods across systems (16:06)
• PAX8 Integration Nuances highlighted hourly sync cycles but infrequent updates to customer-product mappings, causing duplicate or stale records from deleted customers to persist (41:18)
    ◦ Carlos discovered duplicates in PAX8 customer data, including deleted entries still syncing, leading to confusion; Andrew noted the need for the data engineering team to remove stale links (53:17)
    ◦ The team agreed renaming and clarifying addresses in Rev IO and PAX8 would help distinguish active vs deleted records (58:15)
• Mapping and Auto-Mapping Issues remain a blocker for smooth integration, with GL code matching introduced as a new method but still requiring manual oversight (21:52)
    ◦ Andrew explained GL code support in the update, which attempts to link products by matching accounting codes, but product names and descriptions need correction for success (22:00)
    ◦ Carlos found that many QBO products, especially legacy and unused ones, clutter the catalog and should be deactivated to improve mapping accuracy (33:45)
• Integration UI and Workflow Improvements are needed to allow easier reconfiguration, viewing of mappings by integration, and clearer status indicators (32:24)
    ◦ Filtering by integration source (QBO or PAX8) and unmapped items helps but the interface is currently confusing and lumps all integrations together (32:16)
    ◦ Andy plans to review GUI glitches and improve the workflow for creating and confirming customer imports (01:05:14)
Invoice and Billing Process Enhancements
The team explored ways to improve invoice details, billing flexibility, and payment handling to better reflect actual service work and payment plans.
• Detailed Time Entry Visibility on Invoices was identified as a key improvement to avoid customer confusion over repeated ticket billing (01:15:26)
    ◦ Carlos wants invoices to show individual time entries with dates and summarized work descriptions, similar to Synchro’s clean format, to clearly itemize billed labor (01:16:33)
    ◦ Mark acknowledged current PSA invoices show only ticket numbers without detailed time entries or dates, and agreed to prioritize exposing time entry dates and work summaries on invoices (01:22:27)
    ◦ The team discussed hiding technician names on invoices as a privacy preference, with Carlos preferring to keep tech details off billing documents (01:23:11)
• Billing Profiles and Payment Plans require enhancements to handle automatic and manual payments distinctly (01:43:16)
    ◦ Carlos wants to assign payment profiles that automate monthly billing for recurring charges while allowing one-offs to be manually invoiced and paid, preventing manual invoice creation for payment plans (01:43:33)
    ◦ Mark confirmed PSA supports automatic invoice generation and emailing but raised questions about how failed payments and back charges would be handled in such plans (01:41:45)
    ◦ Tax handling on invoices was discussed, with Carlos advocating for tax rates to apply based on the invoice service date, not generation date, to reflect pre-announced tax changes accurately (01:44:51)
• Invoice Template and Layout Customization is a work in progress to better format tables and include relevant data (01:13:56)
    ◦ Mark shared a draft invoice template incorporating ticket numbers and messages but noted time entry details are not yet supported in the preview (01:14:53)
    ◦ Carlos emphasized the need to minimize wasted space and allow the description to float across the invoice, making bill details clearer (01:19:54)
• Invoice Segmentation for Labor and Products to separate billing was requested to avoid mixing charges and improve clarity (01:26:40)
    ◦ Mark explained PSA allows sending invoices filtered by labor or products using the billing function, supporting Carlos’s workflow preference (01:26:48)
Workflow and User Role Controls
Adjustments to system roles, permissions, and ticket workflows were discussed to improve operational control and clarity.
• Restricting Tech Permissions Around Ticket Closure was requested to prevent premature closure of tickets with pending charges (02:10:06)
    ◦ Carlos wants techs to have a “resolved” status instead of “closed” to track tickets needing billing review; Mark confirmed tech role permissions can be adjusted to disable ticket closing (02:10:31)
    ◦ The team agreed that closed tickets currently cannot be billed, reinforcing the need for a separate status for techs (02:09:42)
• Ticket Merging and Time Entry Management was confirmed to merge time entries correctly when tickets are merged (02:12:22)
    ◦ Carlos was reassured that merging tickets consolidates associated time entries, avoiding billing errors (02:12:26)
• Filtering Tickets by Service Teams and Boards to control tech visibility was discussed (01:57:34)
    ◦ Carlos wants to restrict texting technicians from seeing non-tech tickets by assigning service boards to specific teams, but current system lacks multi-board view restrictions (01:58:05)
    ◦ Mark acknowledged the limitation and explored possible workarounds such as creating custom views or permissions (01:57:58)
• Automating Time Entry Defaults and Notes was a priority for simplifying technician input (01:51:59)
    ◦ Carlos requested pre-populated notes and default times for common tasks to reduce manual entry and standardize billing (01:52:04)
    ◦ Mark confirmed PSA currently only auto-fills rates but not notes or times, agreeing to pursue this enhancement (01:54:21)
Product and Inventory Management Improvements
The meeting covered product catalog cleanup, import template corrections, and minor inventory workflow needs.
• Product Catalog Cleanup and Accurate Mapping is critical due to legacy and unused products cluttering the system (33:45)
    ◦ Carlos noted many old NetSuite products remain in QBO and Rev IO, requiring deactivation or removal to improve sync accuracy (34:18)
    ◦ Correcting swapped fields for product name and description in import templates is expected to significantly improve mapping success (37:34)
• Inventory and Vendor Tracking is currently minimal as Carlos rarely uses purchase orders (01:27:28)
    ◦ Mark confirmed vendors can be imported via QuickBooks integration, but Carlos prefers to pull vendor info directly from QuickBooks (01:27:43)
• Adding Products to Tickets and Editing Descriptions was identified as a missing feature (02:04:56)
    ◦ Carlos wants to be able to edit product descriptions once added to tickets to better specify miscellaneous hardware or services (02:05:37)
Sales and Quote Management Updates
The team reviewed new quote and opportunity features designed to improve sales pipeline tracking and quoting flexibility.
• New Opportunity and Proposal Workflow provides a sales pipeline with six stages and ability to add notes and attachments (01:28:18)
    ◦ Mark demonstrated creating opportunities with stages from discovery to verbal agreement, with flexibility to skip stages and track progress (01:29:53)
    ◦ The old quote attachment feature was removed, requiring separate quote editing for detailed proposals (01:30:54)
• Simplified Quick Proposal Creation allows adding products and services outside of quotes, then creating proposals from those items (01:33:13)
    ◦ Mark explained the new flow where products are added first, then a proposal is created and edited, supporting quick one-off quotes that Carlos found useful (01:35:19)
    ◦ Carlos prefers this flow for simple quotes but relies on more detailed templates for complex proposals with multiple service categories (01:35:27)
• Customer Options in Quotes can be included as checkboxes in detailed quote templates but not currently in the new proposal flow (01:37:44)
    ◦ Carlos expressed limited use for customer options, preferring straightforward pricing presentations (01:38:25)
Reporting and Analytics Access
Access to reporting dashboards and creation of new reports was a minor but important topic.
• Reporting Permissions and Dashboard Visibility currently restrict Carlos’s access despite admin rights (02:15:55)
    ◦ Mark found reporting permissions are separate from global admin and adjusted Carlos’s roles to enable analytics access (02:18:18)
    ◦ Carlos noted he lacks some dashboards and requested a report showing all unbilled time entries to track pending billable work (02:15:00)
    ◦ Mark confirmed PSA can create an unbilled charges report similar to Synchro’s existing one, helping billing management (02:15:34)

326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.title: Quick Start with CMP Consulting  on 2026-03-26
326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.notes:
Notes
Email and Ticketing Setup
The team confirmed the proper email configuration is essential for seamless ticketing and communication within the PSA system (00:03).
• Unified Email Usage for Ticket Chains ensures all replies stay linked to the same ticket by using the identical email address for outgoing and incoming messages (00:37).
    ◦ This setup enables automated routing so emails go to the right recipient without creating duplicate tickets.
    ◦ Carlos confirmed inbound and outbound emails sync correctly across sales and operations.
    ◦ Mark emphasized the importance of setting inbound email permissions separately and planned to assist with testing that.
• Ticket Creation and Management Workflow was demonstrated, showing how to add new tickets, assign contacts, and manage followers or groups for notifications (08:03).
    ◦ Tickets default to a new status with medium priority but can be customized per case.
    ◦ Followers on tickets can be assigned to receive notifications without being primary assignees.
• Activity Notes and Emailing within tickets can be flagged for customer visibility or set as internal only, with an option to auto-check email notifications for outgoing messages (10:30).
    ◦ Mark plans to enable a system setting to auto-check this box for easier emailing.
    ◦ Replies to emails will not automatically create new tickets; this was verified during testing.
• Permissions Control for Ticket Status Changes was clarified, allowing restriction so field techs cannot close or delete tickets or notes improperly (30:07).
    ◦ Mark showed how to adjust role permissions to prevent accidental ticket closures before billing.
    ◦ This helps maintain billing integrity and workflow order.
Payment and Billing Integration
The team verified that payment processing via TRX is functional, and explored invoice generation and billing workflows (06:43).
• TRX Payment Verification was confirmed by manual testing of deposits and withdrawals, with small transactions ($0.50 and -$0.28) serving as proof of setup (07:39).
    ◦ Carlos noted receiving the verification transactions, indicating TRX is ready.
    ◦ Mark clarified the deposits were manually triggered but represent successful connection.
• Billing Tab and Invoice Creation allow adding products, parts, labor, and time logs directly to tickets for billing (16:15).
    ◦ Parts and labor can be invoiced together or separately depending on billing needs.
    ◦ Time entry reasons and product types must be correctly configured to appear on invoices.
• Invoice Layout Customization Needs were discussed, focusing on improving the location of summary messages and credits on the invoice (33:53).
    ◦ Carlos prefers summaries at the bottom and wants credits applied only to the specific invoice, not the entire account.
    ◦ Mark agreed and planned changes to font size and layout order for better appearance.
• Tax Calculation Setup requires mapping product catalog items to tax codes, which is pending (36:04).
    ◦ Mark explained that without tax code mapping, taxes do not calculate automatically.
    ◦ Carlos outlined Florida’s complex tax laws, emphasizing labor linked to taxable hardware must also be taxed.
    ◦ Mark will send the tax mapping spreadsheet for Carlos to assign product types accordingly.
Product Catalog and Inventory Management
The conversation covered product catalog usage, labor and non-serialized item management, and inventory system enablement (46:07).
• Product Catalog Usage and Bulk Actions are limited until inventory management is enabled, which is currently off (46:55).
    ◦ Carlos confirmed limited use of packages and purchase orders at this time.
    ◦ Mark explained bulk product actions are possible but disabled without inventory features activated.
• Labor and Product Type Configuration Sync Issues with QuickBooks were identified as a possible cause of lost labor classifications (20:51).
    ◦ Mark suspects QuickBooks auto-mapping labor items to non-serialized types.
    ◦ They plan to verify QuickBooks product types and sync schedules to correct this.
• Time Entry Reasons and Labor Types need proper setup to display on tickets and invoices (19:16).
    ◦ Mark showed how to set labor product types and edit product catalog entries.
    ◦ Carlos wants default time entry assignment to the logged-in user for easier tracking.
• Invoice Display Preferences favor simplicity with essential details only—description, quantity, rate, and total (01:02:11).
    ◦ Carlos prefers not showing start/end times; Mark agreed to streamline invoice lines.
    ◦ Inclusion of a QR code for payment on invoices was approved for customer convenience (01:03:30).
Invoicing and Quote Management
The team reviewed invoice templates, quote creation, and integration with tickets and contracts (01:22:23).
• Invoice Template Customization is ongoing to improve layout and include ticket details at the top and messages at the bottom (56:24).
    ◦ Mark will create a custom invoice template based on Carlos’s sample to enhance clarity.
    ◦ Carlos stressed the value of showing individual charges by ticket and time entry for transparency.
• Quote Creation and Editing allows adding sections like hardware and labor, with options for showing or hiding columns for recipients (01:23:34).
    ◦ Signature fields can be assigned to specific users and set as required.
    ◦ Quotes generate tickets upon signing, enabling seamless transition to billing.
• Recurring Services and Contracts are handled separately from one-time fees, requiring contract activation after quote signing (01:31:29).
    ◦ Mark explained that recurring services appear under contracts, not tickets, and must be manually activated.
    ◦ Carlos plans to address contracts and recurring billing in the next meeting once products are loaded.
• Invoice Line Item Correlation Issues were raised regarding time entries appearing disconnected from tickets and technicians (01:10:40).
    ◦ Carlos highlighted the difficulty in tracking labor lines without ticket or technician association.
    ◦ Mark suggested different product types per technician as a workaround but acknowledged the system’s current limitations.
Process Improvements and Next Steps
The meeting concluded with identification of key focus areas and scheduling follow-up actions (01:33:05).
• Primary Focus on Invoice and Agreement Improvements was agreed upon to finalize product loading and recurring billing workflows (01:33:11).
    ◦ Mark will engage the data engineering team to prepare product data.
    ◦ He will also develop invoice templates tailored to Carlos’s preferences.
• Next Meeting Scheduled for Next Thursday to continue discussions on agreements, contracts, and recurring billing (01:33:47).
    ◦ Carlos confirmed availability; Mark will send calendar invite.
• Ongoing System Enhancements include improved ticket assignment features and email inbound processing (54:59, 10:11).
    ◦ Mark reported that development is underway to allow assigning primary techs from the dispatch board.
    ◦ He plans to assist Carlos with inbound email configuration and testing soon.
• Pending Technical Checks and Sync Validations on QuickBooks integration and tax code mappings remain critical (20:51, 01:18:38).
    ◦ Carlos and Mark will verify QuickBooks sync behaviors to maintain correct labor classifications.
    ◦ Tax mapping spreadsheet will be sent to ensure proper tax handling per Florida law.

326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.title: Quick Start with CMP Consulting  on 2026-03-20
326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.notes:
Had a great call with Carlos to go through system settings/customers/tickets.
Here are the Fireflies notes:

User Roles Customization: Need distinct groups for ticket assignments and access control to improve operations.

Invoice Timing Settings: Carlos prefers invoices generated on the first of the month to avoid tax errors.

QuickBooks Sync Issues: Encountered a 401 error; importance of syncing invoice payment status emphasized. Needs follow up.

Ticketing Workflow Focus: Techs want to filter ticket views, with macros for common tasks proposed to reduce manual entry.

Calendar Integration Needs: Two-way sync with Outlook essential; ensures ticket tasks appear directly on Carlos’s calendar. We got this done!

Email Notifications Improvement: SPF records need updating to enhance email delivery to avoid junk folders.

326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.title: CMP Consulting  Kickoff on 2026-03-20
326a59b7-e7b2-81a8-975f-ccdb7857837c.notes: Great Kick off with Carlos at CMP. He is excited to go quickly, and he wants to learn about the API aspect of PSA. Scheduled a quick start session for Friday 3/20.
